skill.name: code-refactoring
skill.description: Refactor code in small, behavior-preserving steps using tests, focused transformations, and clear validation checklists.
skill.summary: Improve code clarity and reduce duplication while preserving existing behavior.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review shows most are false positives from markdown code fences, TypeScript template literals, tags, and documentation links. The only confirmed concern is that the skill recommends running local shell commands for tests, type checks, linting, and multi-agent verification, which is legitimate for refactoring but requires user review before execution.

medium_findings.title: User-Directed Shell Command Guidance
medium_findings.description: The skill includes bash examples for npm tests, TypeScript checks, linting, snapshot updates, ask-gemini, and codex-cli shell. These commands support legitimate validation, but an agent could execute project scripts, so users should review commands before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and semantically relevant to code refactoring validation. Risk is medium because they execute local project tooling, but no malicious payload or secret exfiltration is present.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded Reference URLs
low_findings.description: The markdown reference section links to refactoring.com, an OReilly book page, and Wikipedia. These are informational links, not automatic network requests or data exfiltration.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The evidence is plain markdown links in a references section. No code path fetches these URLs or transmits local data.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Misread as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The related-skill links use ../ and ../../ to point to nearby markdown documentation. They are not filesystem read or write operations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The path traversal sequence appears only inside markdown links. There is no executable code that uses those paths for file access.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto Matches Are Keyword False Positives
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography alerts correspond to words such as description, design, DRY, and SOLID in metadata, checklist text, and tags. No cryptographic algorithm is implemented or recommended.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are descriptive text and tags. I found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or similar cryptographic operation in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Backtick Execution Matches Are Markdown and Template Literals
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are markdown code fences or TypeScript template literals used in examples. They are not Ruby backtick execution in an executable Ruby file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is markdown documentation, and the backticks delimit examples or tags. The only real execution guidance is captured separately as user-directed shell commands.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local Shell Command Execution Examples
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill suggests test and verification commands that may run package scripts from the target repository. This is expected for coding workflows but should remain under user control.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are present in bash examples and could execute local scripts. They do not include suspicious remote download, credential access, or destructive flags.

content.value_statement: Complex code is hard to change without regressions. This skill guides behavior-preserving refactors with tests, focused steps, and validation checklists.
actual_capabilities: Breaks long functions into smaller named methods.
Identifies duplicated logic and extracts shared helpers.
Suggests polymorphism for complex conditional branches.
Groups long parameter lists into parameter objects.
Uses checklists to preserve behavior during refactoring.
Recommends tests, type checks, and lint checks after changes.
limitations: It does not prove behavior preservation without tests or runtime checks.
It provides guidance, not an automated refactoring engine.
Large architectural changes still need human design review.
Suggested shell commands may differ by project tooling.

use_cases.title: Clean Up a Complex Function
use_cases.description: Split a long function into smaller units while keeping the same inputs, outputs, and side effects.

use_cases.title: Reduce Technical Debt Before Feature Work
use_cases.description: Refactor duplicated code before adding new behavior so future changes stay smaller and clearer.

use_cases.title: Review Refactoring Safety
use_cases.description: Check whether proposed changes are small, tested, and aligned with behavior-preservation rules.

prompt_templates.title: Find Simple Refactors
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner scan for safe cleanup opportunities.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this file for low-risk refactoring opportunities. Focus on duplication, naming, function length, and behavior preservation.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Methods Safely
prompt_templates.scenario: Turn one long function into smaller functions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this long function using Extract Method. Keep behavior unchanged and list the tests needed to verify the change.

prompt_templates.title: Replace Conditional Logic
prompt_templates.scenario: Improve a repeated branch or strategy selector.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this conditional logic and propose a polymorphic or strategy-based refactor. Explain migration steps and regression risks.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Multi-Step Refactor
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an incremental refactoring plan for this module. Include behavior analysis, commit boundaries, verification commands, and rollback points.

output_examples.input: A checkout function validates input, calculates totals, checks inventory, and writes the order in one block.
output: A behavior analysis that lists inputs, outputs, side effects, and invariants.
A refactoring plan that extracts validation, total calculation, inventory checks, and persistence into separate functions.
A verification checklist covering unit tests, type checks, linting, and unchanged error handling.

output_examples.input: Two data access functions repeat the same active-user filters with one small difference.
output: A shared helper or filter object that removes duplicated query conditions.
A short risk note about preserving default behavior and query results.
Suggested tests for the common case and the specialized filter case.

output_examples.input: A payment processor has a long method with branches for several payment methods.
output: A recommendation to isolate each payment method behind a common interface.
A migration sequence that keeps the public processor API stable.
A review checklist for unsupported methods, error handling, and test coverage.

best_practices: Write or identify tests before changing structure.
Refactor one behavior-preserving step at a time.
Run project validation after each meaningful change.
anti_patterns: Do not combine refactoring with feature changes.
Do not introduce abstractions without reducing real complexity.
Do not trust a refactor that lacks behavior checks.

faq.question: Does this skill change application behavior?
faq.answer: No. It is designed for structure changes that preserve existing behavior.

faq.question: Can it run tests automatically?
faq.answer: It can recommend test commands, but users should review commands before execution.

faq.question: Which tools does it support?
faq.answer: The report lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.

faq.question: Is it useful without existing tests?
faq.answer: It is less safe without tests. Add characterization tests before deeper refactoring.

faq.question: What refactoring patterns are covered?
faq.answer: It covers Extract Method, removing duplication, parameter objects, polymorphism, and SOLID-oriented separation.

faq.question: Can it handle large codebases?
faq.answer: It can guide incremental planning, but large changes still need careful review and project-specific validation.
